question_text: If P(x, y, z) is the point nearest to the origin lying on the intersection of the surfaces z = xy + 5 and x + y + z = 1, then the distance (in integer) between the origin and P is
solution:
Minimize f(x, y, z) = x2 + y2 + z2 subject to
g1(x, y, z) = z −xy −5 = 0,
g2(x, y, z) = x + y + z −1 = 0.
Use Lagrange multipliers: ∇f = λ∇g1 + µ∇g2.
(2x, 2y, 2z) = λ(−y, −x, 1) + µ(1, 1, 1).
From the first two coordinates,
2x + λy = µ,
2y + λx = µ ⇒(2 −λ)(x −y) = 0.
If x = y, then from constraints z = 1 −2x and z = x2 + 5 give x2 + 2x + 4 = 0, impossible;
hence λ = 2. Then from the third coordinate 2z = λ + µ = 2 + µ ⇒µ = 2z −2. Using
2x + 2y = µ gives x + y = z −1. With x + y + z = 1 we get z = 1 and x + y = 0. Using
z = xy + 5 yields xy = −4; together with y = −x gives x = ±2, y = ∓2.
Thus P is (2, −2, 1) or (−2, 2, 1) and
∥OP∥=
22 + (−2)2 + 12 =
9 = 3 .
Quick Tip
On intersections of smooth surfaces, minimize ∥x∥2 with Lagrange multipliers. Sym-
metry checks can quickly rule out candidate cases.
